output.summary: Migrate Hero and PostHero gradient styling from hero-specific CSS in colors.css to block-specific Hero.css using the generic gradient system (--gradient-left/--gradient-right). Fix gradient page overflow via overflow-y:clip on .wp-site-blocks wrapper while preserving overflow-visible on the hero section for gradient bleed effect. Add spacer blocks between DemoContainers in test page.

investigation.currentGradientImpl: The Hero block uses CSS class 'hero' on its <section> element and 'top-gradient' on a child div. In colors.css (lines 143-206): (1) .hero defines three custom properties: --gradient-main, --gradient-circle-left, --gradient-circle-right. (2) Per-accent-color overrides exist for .hero.neon-green, .hero.blue, .hero.green, .hero.yellow, .hero.orange, .hero.purple (and their .color .hero variants). (3) .top-gradient applies `absolute top-0 left-0 w-full h-[150vw] -z-10` with a 3-layer background: a linear-gradient using --gradient-main + off-white, and two radial-gradient circles using --gradient-circle-left and --gradient-circle-right. The hero section uses 'overflow-visible' to allow the gradient to bleed into subsequent blocks.
investigation.genericGradientSystem: colors.css (lines 208-237) defines a generic gradient system with :root variables --gradient-left and --gradient-right, plus per-accent-color overrides (.blue, .yellow, .orange, .purple, .green). These map to the same color pairs as the hero-specific --gradient-circle-left and --gradient-circle-right. --gradient-main always equals --accent-color. The hero-specific variables are redundant with the generic system.
investigation.pageGrowthCause: CONFIRMED via DOM measurement at 1440px viewport: The .top-gradient element is 2160px tall (150vw) and absolutely positioned. The last hero block starts at Y=10025px, so its gradient extends to Y=12185px. The footer ends at Y=10773px. The document scrollHeight equals 12185px -- exactly matching the gradient bottom. This means the gradient adds 1412px of extra scrollable whitespace below the footer. The root cause: absolutely positioned elements with overflow-visible parents extend the document scrollable area because no ancestor clips them. The hero MUST keep overflow-visible for the gradient bleed effect, so the fix must be applied at a higher level.
investigation.overflowFixApproach: Add overflow-y:clip to the .wp-site-blocks wrapper element. This element wraps all page content (header + main + footer). Its height is determined by in-flow content only (the absolute gradient does not contribute to its height). overflow-y:clip clips the gradient at the footer's bottom edge, preventing document growth. The gradient still bleeds freely between blocks WITHIN .wp-site-blocks because they are all children of the same container. Key advantages of overflow:clip over overflow:hidden: (1) Does NOT create a scroll container. (2) Does NOT create a new block formatting context. (3) Does NOT affect fixed/sticky positioned elements (header stays fixed). (4) Simply clips painting at the box boundary. This is a site-wide fix in body.css -- not hero-specific -- since any absolutely positioned element with overflow-visible parents could cause the same issue.
investigation.postHeroComparison: PostHero uses class 'post-hero hero' on its <section> with 'overflow-visible' and a 'top-gradient' child div. It inherits all .hero and .top-gradient CSS. PostHero will benefit from: (1) The overflow-y:clip fix on .wp-site-blocks (no changes to PostHero itself for overflow). (2) The gradient CSS moved to Hero.css (PostHero inherits via the shared 'hero' class). No PostHero-specific file changes are needed for the gradient migration or overflow fix.

heroChanges.approach: 1. Create blocks/Hero/Hero.css with the gradient rendering rules moved from colors.css. Replace hero-specific variables with the generic system. 2. Remove ALL hero-specific gradient code from colors.css (lines 143-206). The generic gradient system (lines 208-237) stays. 3. Do NOT change overflow-visible on the hero section -- the gradient bleed effect must be preserved. 4. Fix the page growth issue by adding overflow-y:clip to .wp-site-blocks in body.css. 5. No changes to Hero.php or Hero.tsx are needed for this revision (overflow-visible stays, no attribute changes).

fileChanges.description: Create block-specific CSS for Hero gradient styling
Remove ALL hero-specific gradient code from colors.css
fileChanges.details: Remove lines 143-206: the .hero custom properties block (--gradient-main, --gradient-circle-left, --gradient-circle-right), all six per-color .hero.X / .X .hero overrides, and the .top-gradient @apply rule. These are now in blocks/Hero/Hero.css using the generic system. The generic gradient system (lines 208-237 with --gradient-left/--gradient-right) stays untouched.

fileChanges.description: Add overflow-y:clip to .wp-site-blocks to prevent gradient page overflow
fileChanges.details: Add a new rule: .wp-site-blocks { overflow-y: clip; } This clips absolutely positioned elements (like .top-gradient) that extend beyond the page content area, preventing document growth past the footer. The gradient still bleeds between blocks within .wp-site-blocks. overflow:clip does not create a scroll container and does not affect fixed/sticky elements. This is a site-wide fix -- not hero-specific -- that prevents any overflowing absolute element from growing the page.

postHeroChanges.approach: PostHero requires NO file changes for this revision. It already uses class='post-hero hero' which inherits the .top-gradient rules from Hero.css via the shared 'hero' class (webpack bundles all block CSS into one stylesheet). The overflow-y:clip fix on .wp-site-blocks applies globally and fixes PostHero's gradient overflow as well. PostHero keeps overflow-visible.
postHeroChanges.note: No PostHero file changes needed. The Hero.css gradient rules apply to PostHero via the shared 'hero' class. The body.css overflow fix applies site-wide.

testPageChanges.approach: Add a core/spacer block with 100vh height after each hero block but before the closing DemoContainer tag, for all DemoContainers except the last one. The spacer sits INSIDE the DemoContainer so the gradient (which bleeds beyond the hero via overflow-visible) is visible within the spacer area, giving each hero variation visual breathing room. The last DemoContainer has no spacer since there's no subsequent hero to separate from.
testPageChanges.placement: After each <!-- /wp:takt/hero --> and before <!-- /wp:takt/demo-container --> closing tag, for all DemoContainers EXCEPT the last one
testPageChanges.contentFileStatus: Already updated with spacer blocks from previous plan revision

cssArchitecture.summary: After this change: (1) colors.css owns ONLY the generic gradient variables (--gradient-left, --gradient-right) and per-accent-color overrides -- no hero-specific code. (2) Hero.css owns the .top-gradient rendering rules using generic variables. (3) Both Hero and PostHero blocks use class='hero' so both inherit Hero.css rules. (4) body.css owns the overflow-y:clip on .wp-site-blocks to prevent page growth. (5) Hero and PostHero keep overflow-visible for gradient bleed.

heroCSS.rationale: Replaced --gradient-circle-left with --gradient-left and --gradient-circle-right with --gradient-right from the generic system. Replaced --gradient-main with --accent-color directly (they were always identical). Eliminated all 6 per-color .hero.X overrides -- the generic system already handles per-color variable switching. Uses vanilla CSS (not @apply) per block-specific CSS convention.

designDecisions.question: How to prevent gradient page overflow while preserving overflow-visible on the hero?
designDecisions.recommendation: Add overflow-y:clip to .wp-site-blocks in body.css. This clips the gradient at the page content boundary (after footer) while allowing it to bleed freely between blocks within the page. overflow:clip does not create a scroll container, does not affect fixed/sticky elements, and does not create a new block formatting context. This is the least invasive site-wide fix.
designDecisions.evidence: DOM measurements at 1440px: scrollHeight=12185, footerBottom=10773, gradientBottom=12185. The gradient extends 1412px past the footer. overflow-y:clip on .wp-site-blocks would clip at footerBottom, eliminating the excess.

designDecisions.question: Should PostHero have its own PostHero.css or share Hero.css via the 'hero' class?
designDecisions.recommendation: Share via the 'hero' class. PostHero already uses class='post-hero hero', so it inherits .top-gradient rules from Hero.css. No duplication needed.

designDecisions.question: Where should the spacer blocks go -- inside or outside DemoContainer?
designDecisions.recommendation: Inside DemoContainer, after the hero block. The gradient bleeds from the hero (overflow-visible) into the spacer area, making the gradient effect visible. This is why the user specified 'inside DemoContainer so gradient is visible'.
